claim: Single agents are a ceiling; multi-agent teams with hierarchical orchestration (orchestrator -> leads -> workers) outperform solo coding agents
actionable: Transition from single Claude Code instance to multi-team architecture with domain-specific workers and delegation-enforced leads
source_title: One Agent Is NOT ENOUGH: Agentic Coding BEYOND Claude Code

claim: Three-tier agent hierarchy (orchestrator -> team leads -> workers) with domain locking prevents agents from stepping outside their expertise boundary
actionable: Enforce domain permissions on agents so they can't access/modify files outside their expertise; leads delegate to workers when blocked
source_title: One Agent Is NOT ENOUGH: Agentic Coding BEYOND Claude Code

claim: Agent expertise files (mental models) automatically loaded on startup grow over sessions, compounding knowledge over time unlike single one-off conversations
actionable: Create mental model files for each agent that update after every successful session; agents load these on startup to retain learned context
source_title: One Agent Is NOT ENOUGH: Agentic Coding BEYOND Claude Code

claim: Prompt routing classifiers reduce expensive model calls by routing simple prompts to cheaper models, preserving budget for complex reasoning
actionable: Build prompt complexity classifiers that route to Haiku/Sonnet for simple tasks and reserve Opus for high-reasoning work
source_title: One Agent Is NOT ENOUGH: Agentic Coding BEYOND Claude Code

claim: Zero micromanagement skill enforces that team leads delegate all execution work and only handle planning/coordination, never direct file changes except mental models
actionable: Create a reusable 'zero micromanagement' skill that blocks leads from doing worker tasks; force delegation through system prompt constraints
source_title: One Agent Is NOT ENOUGH: Agentic Coding BEYOND Claude Code

claim: Multi-agent consensus on decisions (e.g., 3 teams recommending same classifier) is a signal of high-confidence recommendations in production systems
actionable: Ask multiple specialized teams the same question and treat unanimous or strong-consensus answers as high-confidence decisions
source_title: One Agent Is NOT ENOUGH: Agentic Coding BEYOND cyber Code

claim: Configuration-based agent teams allow rapid recomposition; change YAML config to add/remove teams without rewriting system prompts
actionable: Use YAML or JSON config files to define agent teams, expertise, domain rules, and skills; iterate teams by editing config not code
source_title: One Agent Is NOT ENOUGH: Agentic Coding BEYOND Claude Code

claim: Injecting variables at runtime into system prompts (session directory, team list, expertise files) creates dynamic, context-aware agent behavior
actionable: Template system prompts with runtime variables for session context, expertise paths, and team definitions instead of hardcoding
source_title: One Agent Is NOT ENOUGH: Agentic Coding BEYOND Claude Code

claim: Specialized agents with different model tiers (Opus for leads/orchestrator, Sonnet for workers) optimize cost while maintaining quality for complex tasks
actionable: Use weaker models for workers executing known tasks and reserve strongest models for orchestration and complex decision-making
source_title: One Agent Is NOT ENOUGH: Agentic Coding BEYOND Claude Code

claim: 1 million context window for agents enables them to load full expertise files and conversation history, allowing specialized mental models to grow over time
actionable: Leverage large context windows to store agent expertise and domain knowledge directly in system prompts without token waste
source_title: One Agent Is NOT ENOUGH: Agentic Coding BEYOND Claude Code

claim: Active listener skill (reading conversation log before every response) allows all agents to maintain awareness of multi-agent decisions and consensus
actionable: Add active listener skill to agents that need cross-team awareness; skip it for independent workers to reduce context overhead
source_title: One Agent Is NOT ENOUGH: Agentic Coding BEYOND Claude Code
